title: Angular Fundamentals
summary: Modules, components, templates, and the Angular lifecycle — how Angular bootstraps and renders your application.
guide:
# Angular Fundamentals Angular is an **opinionated, full-framework** approach to building web apps. Where React gives you a rendering library and lets you choose everything else, Angular ships with routing, forms, HTTP, DI, and state management conventions built in. The mental model: Angular is an **assembly line** — every piece is in a defined place with a defined job. ``` Browser loads index.html │ ▼ main.ts → bootstrapApplication(AppComponent) │ ▼ AppComponent (root) ← replaces in HTML / \ NavComp RouterOutlet │ (active route's component) ``` ## NgModules vs Standalone Components (modern Angular) ``` CLASSIC (Angular <14) MODERN (Angular 17+) ┌─────────────────────┐ ┌─────────────────────┐ │ @NgModule │ │ @Component │ │ declarations: [C] │ vs │ standalone: true │ │ imports: [M] │ │ imports: [NgIf,…] │ │ providers: [S] │ │ (self-contained) │ └─────────────────────┘ └─────────────────────┘ Modules group components. Components declare their Required wiring for each. own deps. Simpler mental model. ``` ```ts // Modern standalone component @Component({ selector: 'app-user-card', standalone: true, imports: [NgIf, NgFor, DatePipe], // bring in what you need template: `

{{ user.name }}

{{ user.role }}

`, }) export class UserCardComponent { @Input() user!: User; // required input } ``` ## Component Anatomy ``` @Component decorator │ ├── selector: 'app-xyz' ← the HTML tag name: ├── template / templateUrl ├── styles / styleUrls └── standalone, imports Class body ├── @Input() props ← data IN from parent ├── @Output() EventEmitters ← events OUT to parent ├── Injected services ← constructor(private svc: MyService) └── Lifecycle hooks ← ngOnInit, ngOnDestroy, etc. ``` ```ts @Component({ selector: 'app-counter', standalone: true, template: ` {{ count }} ` }) export class CounterComponent { count = 0; increment() { this.count++; } } ``` ## Templates — Angular's DSL Angular templates are HTML with a superset of expressions and directives: ```html

Hello, {{ user.name }}

  • {{ item.name }}
    • Admin panel
    @if (isAdmin) {
    Admin panel
    } @for (item of items; track item.id) {
  • {{ item.name }}
    • } ``` ## Lifecycle Hooks — component birth → death ``` new Component() │ ▼ ngOnChanges() ← input values first set / changed │ ▼ ngOnInit() ← component initialised, safe to use @Input values │ ▼ ngAfterViewInit() ← template + child components rendered │ [change detection loop] │ ▼ ngOnDestroy() ← cleanup: unsubscribe, clear timers ``` ```ts export class DataComponent implements OnInit, OnDestroy { private sub!: Subscription; ngOnInit() { this.sub = this.dataService.data$.subscribe(d => this.data = d); } ngOnDestroy() { this.sub.unsubscribe(); // prevent memory leak } } ``` ## Dependency Injection — services wired automatically Angular's DI container builds and injects services. You declare what you need, Angular provides it. ``` providedIn: 'root' ← singleton, available app-wide providers: [MyService] ← in component/module metadata AppComponent │ constructor(private http: HttpClient) │ ▼ DI Container → finds HttpClientModule provider → gives instance ``` ```ts @Injectable({ providedIn: 'root' }) export class UserService { constructor(private http: HttpClient) {} getUser(id: number): Observable { return this.http.get(`/api/users/${id}`); } } // Component receives it via constructor — no manual instantiation @Component({…}) export class ProfileComponent { constructor(private users: UserService) {} ngOnInit() { this.users.getUser(1).subscribe(u => this.user = u); } } ``` ## Change Detection — how Angular knows to update the DOM ``` Event / timer / HTTP response fires │ ▼ Zone.js intercepts (classic Angular) │ ▼ Angular runs change detection from root downward through ALL components │ ▼ Compares old vs new template expressions │ ▼ Patches only changed DOM nodes OnPush strategy: only checks when • @Input reference changes • An event fires inside the component • An Observable emits (with async pipe) ``` ## Complexity Cheatsheet | Concept | Key rule | |---|---| | selector | The HTML tag: `` maps to `selector: 'app-name'` | | @Input | Receive data from parent | | @Output + EventEmitter | Send events to parent | | ngOnInit | First safe place to use @Input values (constructor is too early) | | ngOnDestroy | Always unsubscribe from Observables here | | providedIn: 'root' | Singleton service, tree-shakeable | ## Common Pitfalls - Accessing Angular `@Input()` values in the constructor — they're not set yet; use `ngOnInit` - Forgetting to unsubscribe in `ngOnDestroy` — causes memory leaks and ghost callbacks - Using index as `trackBy` in `*ngFor` — same bug as React index keys - Not importing standalone component dependencies in `imports: []` — template fails silently in dev - Using `InMemoryWebApiModule` in production — a testing utility accidentally left in ## Connections - **angular-data-binding** — deep dive into `[]`, `()`, `[()]` binding syntax - **angular-services-di** — hierarchical injectors, component-scoped services - **angular-signals** — modern change detection without Zone.js - **ts-fundamentals** — TypeScript underpins every Angular class

questions.prompt: In Angular, the `selector: 'app-user'` in @Component means:
choices: The component file must be named app-user.ts
The component renders when Angular finds <app-user> in a template
The component is only available in AppModule
The CSS selector that styles the component
questions.explanation: The selector is a CSS-like selector that Angular matches against HTML. When it finds `<app-user>` in a template, it replaces that element with the component's rendered template.

questions.prompt: Why should @Input() values NOT be accessed in the constructor?
choices: Constructor is private in Angular components
Input values are not set until after the constructor runs — Angular sets them between construction and ngOnInit
Constructors cannot have TypeScript logic
Only ngOnChanges can access them
questions.explanation: Angular instantiates the class (constructor runs) then sets @Input properties. By the time ngOnInit runs, all @Input values are ready. Reading @Input in the constructor will get `undefined`.

questions.prompt: What template syntax sends a click event to the parent?
choices: A — square brackets bind events
B — parentheses bind events (event binding syntax)
Both work the same way
Neither — use @HostListener
questions.explanation: In Angular templates: `[]` = property binding (data in). `()` = event binding (event out). `[()]` = two-way binding. Square brackets pass values to a child; parentheses listen for outputs from a child.

questions.prompt: The lifecycle hook `ngOnDestroy` is the right place to unsubscribe from Observables because:
choices: Angular automatically unsubscribes for you after this hook
It runs just before the component is removed from the DOM — the last chance to clean up
It's called on every change detection cycle
Only ngOnDestroy can call .unsubscribe()
questions.explanation: Active subscriptions hold references to the component. If the component is destroyed but the subscription lives on, the callback still fires on the dead component (ghost callback) and the memory is never freed.

questions.prompt: `providedIn: 'root'` on a service means:
choices: The service is only available in the root module
Angular creates one singleton instance app-wide and tree-shakes it if unused
The service must be re-declared in each module that uses it
It makes the service a root component
questions.explanation: `providedIn: 'root'` registers the service with the root injector lazily. One instance is shared everywhere. If nothing injects it, the bundler can remove it (tree-shaking). No need to add it to any `providers` array.

questions.prompt: Which lifecycle hooks commonly need implementation in a data-fetching component?
choices: ngOnInit — start the data fetch after inputs are ready
ngOnDestroy — unsubscribe to prevent memory leaks
ngAfterViewInit — required to render templates
ngOnChanges — react to input value updates
questions.explanation: ngAfterViewInit is for interacting with child component instances / DOM refs — not required for data fetching. ngOnInit, ngOnDestroy, and ngOnChanges are the trio for data-driven components.

questions.prompt: What is wrong with this code?
choices: Nothing — standalone components auto-import NgFor
*ngFor requires NgModule — cannot use with standalone
NgFor must be imported in the imports array of the standalone component
The template format is wrong
questions.explanation: Standalone components must explicitly import all directives they use. `NgFor` must be in `imports: [NgFor]` (or `CommonModule`). Without it, `*ngFor` is an unknown attribute and Angular throws an error.

questions.prompt: Angular's Zone.js (classic change detection) works by:
choices: Manually calling detectChanges() after every operation
Patching browser async APIs (setTimeout, promises, events) to notify Angular after they complete
Polling the DOM every 100ms
Observing property changes via Proxy
questions.explanation: Zone.js monkey-patches async APIs. When a setTimeout callback runs, Zone.js intercepts the completion and tells Angular 'something may have changed — run change detection'. This is why async code automatically triggers updates.

questions.prompt: OnPush change detection strategy tells Angular to only check a component when:
choices: Any variable in the app changes
An @Input reference changes, an event fires inside, or an async pipe emits a new value
The developer calls markForCheck() only
The component is newly created
questions.explanation: OnPush skips the component during global change detection unless one of three things happens: an @Input gets a new reference, an event originates within the component tree, or an Observable subscribed via `async` pipe emits. This forces immutable data patterns.

questions.prompt: Given this template, what renders when `isAdmin` is false?
choices: Nothing — both are hidden when isAdmin is false

Only `

Public content

` — the div is removed from DOM
Admin panel with display:none
An error — ngIf requires else clause
questions.explanation:
`*ngIf` is a structural directive — it adds/removes DOM nodes. When false, the `
` is completely absent from the DOM (not hidden). The `

` is independent and always renders. `else` is optional.

questions.prompt: Two-way binding `[(ngModel)]` is shorthand for:
choices: [ngModel] and (ngModel)Change — property binding + event binding combined
Just property binding — the name is misleading
A special Angular directive only for forms
DOM event listener + state synchronisation
questions.explanation: `[(x)]` is Angular's 'banana-in-a-box' syntax: a property binding `[x]` + an event binding `(xChange)`. For ngModel: `[ngModel]="val"` sets the value and `(ngModelChange)="val=$event"` updates it on change.

questions.prompt: The difference between Angular's `@Input()` and React's props is:
choices: No difference — both are immutable read-only values
Angular @Input is mutable by the child; React props are strictly read-only by convention
@Input() values are typed; React props are not
@Input() is only for primitive types
questions.explanation: Angular does not enforce immutability of @Input values. You CAN mutate an object passed via @Input — Angular will not warn you. React enforces props as read-only by convention. Both patterns work similarly architecturally: parent→child data flow.

questions.prompt: In the new Angular control flow syntax, what replaces `*ngFor`?
choices:
@for (item of items; track item.id) {
  • {{item.name}}
    • }

#for item in items {
  • {{item.name}}
    • }
v-for="item in items"
{items.map(item => <li>...)}
questions.explanation: Angular 17 introduced built-in control flow: `@if`, `@for`, `@switch`. The `@for` requires a `track` expression (like trackBy) which is now mandatory, not optional — encouraging correct performance practice.

questions.prompt: What happens if you forget to call `.unsubscribe()` in ngOnDestroy?
choices: Nothing — Angular cleans up all subscriptions automatically
The Observable completes on its own after component destruction
The subscription callback keeps firing on the destroyed component — memory leak and ghost state updates
TypeScript compiler warns about it
questions.explanation: An active subscription holds a reference to the component class, preventing garbage collection. If the Observable emits again (HTTP polling, WebSocket, interval), it tries to update state on a destroyed component — often causing 'ExpressionChangedAfterItHasBeenChecked' errors or silent state corruption.

questions.prompt: Which are differences between NgModule-based and standalone Angular components?
choices: Standalone components declare their own imports instead of relying on a shared NgModule
Standalone components cannot use Angular features like HttpClient
NgModules are required in Angular 17+ for lazy loading
Standalone components enable better tree-shaking and simpler mental model
questions.explanation: Standalone components CAN use all Angular features — they just import them directly. NgModules are NOT required in Angular 17+; standalone-based lazy loading works via `loadComponent`. Standalone = simpler, more explicit, better tree-shaking.

questions.prompt: The `async` pipe in an Angular template:
choices: Makes the template asynchronous
Subscribes to an Observable, unwraps its value into the template, and automatically unsubscribes on component destroy
Converts a Promise to a synchronous value
Is required for all HTTP calls
questions.explanation: The async pipe handles the subscribe/unsubscribe lifecycle automatically. It also triggers OnPush change detection when a new value arrives. It is the most common way to avoid manual subscription management in templates.

questions.prompt: What does `@Output() clicked = new EventEmitter<string>()` allow the component to do?
choices: Push data INTO the component from a parent
Emit a string event that the parent can listen to with (clicked)="handler($event)"
Subscribe to a global event bus
Mutate a parent component's state directly
questions.explanation: @Output + EventEmitter is Angular's way for a child to notify its parent. The parent uses event binding syntax `(clicked)="myHandler($event)"` to receive the emitted string. This closes the loop of parent→child data and child→parent events.

questions.prompt: Angular Dependency Injection resolves dependencies using:
choices: Variable names in the constructor
TypeScript constructor parameter types — the type annotation IS the injection token (with metadata)
Property decorators only
A global service locator singleton
questions.explanation: Angular reads the TypeScript constructor parameter types using emitted metadata (`emitDecoratorMetadata: true`). `constructor(private http: HttpClient)` — Angular sees `HttpClient` and looks it up in its DI container. The type annotation = the DI token.

questions.prompt: Why is `trackBy` (or `track` in new control flow) important with `*ngFor`?
choices: Required for compile — template fails without it
Tells Angular what makes each item unique so it reuses existing DOM nodes when the array changes instead of destroying and recreating all nodes
Sorts the list before rendering
Prevents duplicate items from rendering
questions.explanation: Without trackBy, any array change causes Angular to destroy and recreate all DOM nodes in the list (expensive). With trackBy returning a stable id, Angular matches old nodes to new items by identity — only new items get new DOM nodes.

questions.prompt: Angular's dependency injection hierarchy means a service provided at the component level:
choices: Is shared with all components in the app
Gets a new instance scoped to that component and its descendants
Is the same instance as the root-provided service
Cannot be injected into child components
questions.explanation: Angular's DI is hierarchical (root → module → component). Providing a service at the component level creates a new instance for that component subtree. Child components get this instance, not the root singleton. This enables isolated, test-friendly service scope.

flashcards.back: The HTML tag that triggers this component: `selector: 'app-hero'` → use `<app-hero>` in templates. Must be unique per app.
@Input = data IN from parent via `[prop]` binding. @Output + EventEmitter = event OUT to parent via `(event)` binding. Together they form the component contract.
Constructor: service injection only — @Input not yet set. ngOnInit: first safe place for logic using @Input values. Rule: inject in constructor, initialise in ngOnInit.
Unsubscribe from all Observables/Subjects. Clear setInterval/setTimeout. Disconnect ResizeObserver. Prevents memory leaks and ghost callbacks.
Singleton service available app-wide, tree-shakeable if unused. No need to add to providers array. Use for shared stateless services (HttpClient wrapper, AuthService).

flashcards.front: OnPush change detection — when it checks
flashcards.back: @Input reference changes OR event fires inside component OR async pipe emits. Use as default for all components — forces immutable update patterns.
Subscribes to Observable/Promise in template, unwraps value, auto-unsubscribes on destroy. Works with OnPush. Eliminates manual subscribe/unsubscribe boilerplate.
Every directive, pipe, and component used in the template must be listed here. Not imported → unknown template element → error. Replaces NgModule declarations for standalone.

project.brief: Design a 'Product Catalogue' feature in Angular. It shows a list of products, allows filtering by category, and shows a detail panel on click. 1) Sketch the component tree with selectors shown. 2) Decide which components are standalone and which (if any) use NgModule — justify. 3) For state: where does the selected product live? Where does the filter query live? 4) Identify which lifecycle hooks each component needs and explain why. 5) You have a ProductService that fetches products via HTTP. How do you inject it? How do you unsubscribe safely? 6) Which components should use OnPush and why?

checklist.text: Component tree drawn with selectors and @Input/@Output on edges
State ownership justified — selected product and filter query placement explained
Lifecycle hooks per component identified with reasons (ngOnInit for fetch, ngOnDestroy for cleanup)
ProductService injection and unsubscribe strategy described (async pipe or takeUntilDestroyed)
OnPush strategy applied to at least ProductCard and justified

hints: The filter query belongs in the parent (CatalogueComponent) — it needs to filter before passing to the list.
ProductCard is a pure display component — it receives a product @Input and emits a (selected) event. Perfect OnPush candidate.
Use the `async` pipe in the template instead of subscribing in ngOnInit — it handles unsubscription automatically.
takeUntilDestroyed(this.destroyRef) is the modern Angular way to auto-unsubscribe without ngOnDestroy.
